title: Git Internals
description: Git is fundamentally a content-addressable object store. Every file, directory, and commit is an object identified by its SHA-1 hash.

sections.title: The Object Store
sections.description:
All Git objects live in .git/objects/, stored by SHA-1 hash (first 2 chars = directory, remaining 38 = filename). Objects are zlib-compressed. Use git cat-file -p to inspect any object by hash. Every object has a type header: blob, tree, commit, or tag.

git cat-file -p HEAD          # inspect commit
git hash-object file.txt      # compute SHA-1
git ls-tree HEAD              # list tree entries

sections.title: Blobs & Trees
sections.description:
A blob stores file content — just the bytes, no name or metadata. A tree represents a directory: it maps filenames to blob SHAs, with mode (100644 = file, 100755 = executable, 040000 = subdirectory). Trees can reference other trees (subdirectories).

git ls-tree -r HEAD           # recursive tree
git ls-tree -l HEAD           # with object sizes
git write-tree                # write tree from index

sections.title: Commits & Tags
sections.description:
A commit object contains: tree SHA (root directory), parent SHA(s), author/committer (name + email + timestamp), and message. An annotated tag points to a commit and adds a tagger, message, and optional GPG signature. Lightweight tags are just refs.

git cat-file commit HEAD      # raw commit content
git cat-file tag v1.0         # raw tag content
git show HEAD --format=raw    # full commit details

sections.title: References & HEAD
sections.description:
Refs are pointers stored in .git/refs/: heads/ (local branches), remotes/ (remote branches), tags/. HEAD is a symbolic ref pointing to the current branch (e.g., ref: refs/heads/main). Detached HEAD means HEAD points directly to a commit.

git symbolic-ref HEAD         # show HEAD target
git rev-parse HEAD            # resolve to SHA
git rev-parse --symbolic-full-name HEAD

sections.title: Packfiles & GC
sections.description:
Git auto-packs loose objects into packfiles (.idx + .pack) for efficiency. Packfiles use delta compression: similar objects are stored as base + delta chain. git gc runs manually or automatically. It prunes unreachable objects (older than 2 weeks by default) and repacks remaining objects.

git gc --aggressive          # heavy optimization
git count-objects -v         # object statistics
git verify-pack .git/objects/pack/*.idx

details: Git objects are immutable — once created, they never change. The SHA-1 hash is computed as sha1(type + ' ' + size + '\0' + content). This means identical files (same bytes) always produce the same blob SHA, regardless of filename or location — Git automatically deduplicates. The object graph is a Merkle DAG: the commit hash depends on the tree hash, which depends on the blob hashes. This cryptographic integrity means any change to any file at any point in history is detectable. Packfiles can be thin (used during fetch/push) or self-contained. Git's GC uses a generational approach: recent objects are loose; older objects get packed. The --window and --depth parameters control delta search effort.
cheatSheet:
Core Commands

  • git cat-file -p HEAD — inspect any object (commit, tree, blob) by SHA or ref.
  • git ls-tree -r HEAD — recursive tree listing: all files with modes and SHAs.
  • git hash-object file.txt — compute the SHA-1 hash a file would have as a blob.
  • git count-objects -v — object count, packfile info, and GC statistics.

Staff+ Patterns

  • Use git cat-file --batch for efficient bulk object inspection in scripts — avoids one process per object.
  • Understand that identical files deduplicate automatically. This makes Git efficient for monorepos with many similar files (templates, configs).

Watch Outs

  • git gc --aggressive can take hours on large repos and provides marginal benefit. Prefer git gc --auto.
  • Loose objects older than 2 weeks are pruned by git gc. If you need an object, ensure it's reachable from a ref or reflog.

Interview Angle

Google L6 asks about Git hosting design — knowing the object model is table stakes. Explain the Merkle DAG property: because commit hashes depend on tree hashes which depend on blob hashes, any corruption anywhere is detectable. This is why Git is considered a content-addressable, integrity-verified filesystem.
